solutions.solution:
Statements: K @ T, S = K, T ⊕ R
Conclusions: I. S @ B
For conclusion I, all statements are relevant. Combining B ≤ C ≤ D with A > D, We get B ≤ C ≤ D which means B ≤ D. But we can't combine with A > D because both of them B is the smallest and A is the largest (Tip 8 in both cases). Conclusion I is obviously false because C and D are not equal to any thing in the given question. (Tip 7).

Statements: U = M, P ⊕⊕ U, M ⊕ B
Conclusions: I. P = B

Statements: L @ N, J ⊕ P, P ⊕ L
Conclusions: I. P = B

Statements: H @ G, D @ E, H = E
Conclusions: I. D @ N
For I, all statements are relevant. Combining H ≤ G with E ≤ H and E = H, we get K = N. This means H is wrong. But K < N and L ≤ K means that either H ≤ K or K ≤ H. (Check 3)
I is also wrong.

Statements: X > Y, Z ≤ R, Y = Z
Conclusions: I. X > R
Combining X > Y and Y = Z, we get X > Z. Combining this to Z ≤ R, not possible as the tip is pointed toward Z in both cases; so no conclusions about X and R. If is of course wrong as R is not equal to any element in the given statements (Tip 7).

Statements: G ≥ F, D = E, E = F
Conclusions: I. E ≤ G
From G ≥ F and E = F, we get G ≥ E which involves that either E < G or E = G.

Statements: J ≤ J, K = J ≤ L, H ≤ J
Conclusions: I. L = H
There are no equalities involving L in the given statements so we can't be any equality involving L in any conclusion (Tip 7). So, I is wrong. For H, I ≤ J and H ≤ J are relevant. Combining them we get J = H which gives I as true.

Statements: N < W, L > N, K = L, W = D
Conclusions: I. L = W
For I, N < W and L > N are relevant but they can't be combined as W is pointed towards N in both cases. So, I is uncertain. If is true; it follows from D = W > N.
